prompt:
retention.py (attached, in production, fine) loads our snapshot retention policies: ordered `rule NAME match=<pattern> max_age=<Nh|Nd> action=keep|archive|delete` lines, first match wins, optional `default <action>`. Last week a policy shipped where a catch-all `match=*` sat above everything, so three carefully written rules below it were dead weight and a month of database snapshots got kept that should have been archived. A human caught it in review, eventually. I want the machine to catch it: add `lint_policy(policy)` to retention.py. The EXISTING BEHAVIOR block in test_retention.py passes against the shipped file today and must stay green -- load_policy/evaluate semantics are frozen.

lint_policy returns a list of findings, each a dict {'code', 'rule', 'other', 'message', 'suggestion'} where 'rule' is the offending (later) rule's name and 'other' the earlier rule that causes it. Four codes. duplicate-rule: a later rule repeats an earlier one exactly (same pattern, same max_age, same action) -- message "rule '<later>' repeats rule '<earlier>'", suggestion "delete rule '<later>'". conflicting-action: same pattern and same max_age but a different action -- message "rule '<later>' contradicts rule '<earlier>': same scope, action '<later action>' vs '<earlier action>'", suggestion "keep one action for pattern '<pattern>' at <age>h". unreachable-tier: same pattern where the earlier rule's max_age is LARGER, so the later, tighter tier can never fire -- message "rule '<later>' never fires: rule '<earlier>' already covers pattern '<pattern>' up to <earlier age>h", suggestion "order '<pattern>' tiers by increasing max_age". shadowed-rule: a DIFFERENT, broader earlier pattern (remember patterns are literal or literal-prefix-star, so coverage is decidable) matches everything the later one matches and its max_age is >= the later one's -- message "rule '<later>' never fires: rule '<earlier>' matches everything it matches first", suggestion "move rule '<later>' above rule '<earlier>'". Ages in messages are always the stored hours with an h suffix (30d prints as 720h).

Precision matters or people will ignore it: proper tiering (same pattern, INCREASING max_age down the file) is not a finding; a narrower rule above a broader one is not a finding; a broader-but-shorter-lived rule above a longer narrower one is not a finding (the later rule still catches the older ages). Each rule produces at most ONE finding, attributed to the EARLIEST earlier rule responsible, and findings come back in rule-definition order.

Bar: `python3 test_retention.py` fully green (the linting blocks fail today, the existing blocks don't). Test file is read-only. Stdlib only.
